title: Photorealistic Text-to-Image Diffusion Models with Deep Language Understanding [PDF]

## **Photorealistic Text-to-Image Diffusion Models with Deep Language Understanding**

**Chitwan Saharia** _[∗]_ **, William Chan** _[∗]_ **, Saurabh Saxena** _[†]_ **, Lala Li** _[†]_ **, Jay Whang** _[†]_ **, Emily Denton, Seyed Kamyar Seyed Ghasemipour, Burcu Karagol Ayan, S. Sara Mahdavi, Raphael Gontijo-Lopes, Tim Salimans, Jonathan Ho** _[†]_ **, David J Fleet** _[†‡]_ **, Mohammad Norouzi** _[∗]_

```
{sahariac,williamchan,mnorouzi}@google.com
{srbs,lala,jwhang,jonathanho,davidfleet}@google.com
```

Google Research, Brain Team Toronto, Ontario, Canada

## **Abstract**

We present Imagen, a text-to-image diffusion model with an unprecedented degree of photorealism and a deep level of language understanding. Imagen builds on the power of large transformer language models in understanding text and hinges on the strength of diffusion models in high-fidelity image generation. Our key discovery is that generic large language models (e.g. T5), pretrained on text-only corpora, are surprisingly effective at encoding text for image synthesis: increasing the size of the language model in Imagen boosts both sample fidelity and image-text alignment much more than increasing the size of the image diffusion model. Imagen achieves a new state-of-the-art FID score of 7.27 on the COCO dataset, without ever training on COCO, and human raters find Imagen samples to be on par with the COCO data itself in image-text alignment. To assess text-to-image models in greater depth, we introduce DrawBench, a comprehensive and challenging benchmark for text-to-image models. With DrawBench, we compare Imagen with recent methods including VQ-GAN+CLIP, Latent Diffusion Models, GLIDE and DALL-E 2, and find that human raters prefer Imagen over other models in side-by-side comparisons, both in terms of sample quality and image-text alignment.

## **1 Introduction**

Multimodal learning has come into prominence recently, with text-to-image synthesis [55, 12, 59] and image-text contrastive learning [51, 32, 77] at the forefront. These models have transformed the research community and captured widespread public attention with creative image generation [22, 56] and editing applications [21, 43, 36]. To pursue this research direction further, we introduce Imagen, a text-to-image diffusion model that combines the power of transformer language models (LMs) [15, 54] with high-fidelity diffusion models [28, 29, 16, 43] to deliver an unprecedented degree of photorealism and a deep level of language understanding in text-to-image synthesis. In contrast to prior work that uses only image-text data for model training [e.g., 55, 43], the key finding behind Imagen is that text embeddings from large LMs [54, 15], pretrained on text-only corpora, are remarkably effective for text-to-image synthesis. See Fig. 1 for select samples.
Imagen comprises a frozen T5-XXL [54] encoder to map input text into a sequence of embeddings and a 64 _×_ 64 image diffusion model, followed by two super-resolution diffusion models for generating

2

256 _×_ 256 and 1024 _×_ 1024 images (see Fig. A.4). All diffusion models are conditioned on the text embedding sequence and use classifier-free guidance [27]. Imagen relies on new sampling techniques to allow usage of large guidance weights without sample quality degradation observed in prior work, resulting in images with higher fidelity and better image-text alignment than previously possible.

While conceptually simple and easy to train, Imagen yields surprisingly strong results. Imagen outperforms other methods on COCO [38] with zero-shot FID-30K of 7.27, significantly outperforming prior work such as GLIDE [43] (at 12.4) and the concurrent work of DALL-E 2 [56] (at 10.4). Our zero-shot FID score is also better than state-of-the-art models trained on COCO, e.g., Make-A-Scene [22] (at 7.6). Additionally, human raters indicate that generated samples from Imagen are on-par in image-text alignment to the reference images on COCO captions.

We introduce DrawBench, a new structured suite of text prompts for text-to-image evaluation. DrawBench enables deeper insights through a multi-dimensional evaluation of text-to-image models, with text prompts designed to probe different semantic properties of models. These include compositionality, cardinality, spatial relations, the ability to handle complex text prompts or prompts with rare words, and they include creative prompts that push the limits of models’ ability to generate highly implausible scenes well beyond the scope of the training data. With DrawBench, extensive human evaluation shows that Imagen outperforms other recent methods [59, 12, 56] by a significant margin. We further demonstrate some of the clear advantages of the use of large pre-trained language models [54] over multi-modal embeddings such as CLIP [51] as a text encoder for Imagen.

Key contributions of the paper include:

1. We discover that large frozen language models trained only on text data are surprisingly very effective text encoders for text-to-image generation, and that scaling the size of frozen text encoder improves sample quality significantly more than scaling the size of image diffusion model.
2. We introduce _dynamic thresholding_ , a new diffusion sampling technique to leverage high guidance weights and generating more photorealistic and detailed images than previously possible.
3. We highlight several important diffusion architecture design choices and propose _Efficient U-Net_ , a new architecture variant which is simpler, converges faster and is more memory efficient.
4. We achieve a new state-of-the-art COCO FID of 7.27. Human raters find Imagen to be on-par with the reference images in terms of image-text alignment.

5. We introduce DrawBench, a new comprehensive and challenging evaluation benchmark for the text-to-image task. On DrawBench human evaluation, we find Imagen to outperform all other work, including the concurrent work of DALL-E 2 [56].

## **2 Imagen**

Imagen consists of a text encoder that maps text to a sequence of embeddings and a cascade of conditional diffusion models that map these embeddings to images of increasing resolutions (see Fig. A.4). In the following subsections, we describe each of these components in detail.

## **2.1 Pretrained text encoders**

Text-to-image models need powerful semantic text encoders to capture the complexity and compositionality of arbitrary natural language text inputs. Text encoders trained on paired image-text data are standard in current text-to-image models; they can be trained from scratch [43, 55] or pretrained on image-text data [56] (e.g., CLIP [51]). The image-text training objectives suggest that these text encoders may encode visually semantic and meaningful representations especially relevant for the text-to-image generation task. Large language models can be another models of choice to encode text for text-to-image generation. Recent progress in large language models (e.g., BERT [15], GPT [49, 50, 7], T5 [54]) have led to leaps in textual understanding and generative capabilities. Language models are trained on text only corpus significantly larger than paired image-text data, thus being exposed to a very rich and wide distribution of text. These models are also generally much larger than text encoders in current image-text models [51, 32, 83] (e.g. PaLM [11] has 540B parameters, while CoCa [83] has a _≈_ 1B parameter text encoder).

It thus becomes natural to explore both families of text encoders for the text-to-image task. Imagen explores pretrained text encoders: BERT [15], T5 [53] and CLIP [48]. For simplicity, we freeze the weights of these text encoders. Freezing has several advantages such as offline computation of embeddings, resulting in negligible computation or memory footprint during training of the textto-image model. In our work, we find that there is a clear conviction that scaling the text encoder size improves the quality of text-to-image generation. We also find that while T5-XXL and CLIP

3

text encoders perform similarly on simple benchmarks such as MS-COCO, human evaluators prefer T5-XXL encoders over CLIP text encoders in both image-text alignment and image fidelity on DrawBench, a set of challenging and compositional prompts. We refer the reader to Section 4.4 for summary of our findings, and Appendix D.1 for detailed ablations.

## **2.2 Diffusion models and classifier-free guidance**

Here we give a brief introduction to diffusion models; a precise description is in Appendix A. Diffusion models [66, 28, 68] are a class of generative models that convert Gaussian noise into samples from a learned data distribution via an iterative denoising process. These models can be conditional, for example on class labels, text, or low-resolution images [e.g. 16, 29, 62, 61, 78, 43, 56]. ## **2.3 Large guidance weight samplers**

We corroborate the results of recent text-guided diffusion work [16, 43, 56] and find that increasing the classifier-free guidance weight improves image-text alignment, but damages image fidelity producing highly saturated and unnatural images [27]. We find that this is due to a train-test mismatch arising from high guidance weights. At each sampling step _t_ , the **x** -prediction **x** ˆ _[t]_ 0[must be within the same] bounds as training data **x** , i.e. within [ _−_ 1 _,_ 1], but we find empirically that high guidance weights cause **x** -predictions to exceed these bounds. This is a train-test mismatch, and since the diffusion model is iteratively applied on its own output throughout sampling, the sampling process produces unnatural images and sometimes even diverges. To counter this problem, we investigate _static thresholding_ and _dynamic thresholding_ . See Appendix Fig. A.31 for reference implementation of the techniques and Appendix Fig. A.9 for visualizations of their effects.

**Static thresholding** : We refer to elementwise clipping the **x** -prediction to [ _−_ 1 _,_ 1] as _static thresholding_ . This method was in fact used but not emphasized in previous work [28], and to our knowledge its importance has not been investigated in the context of guided sampling. We discover that static thresholding is essential to sampling with large guidance weights and prevents generation of blank images. Nonetheless, static thresholding still results in over-saturated and less detailed images as the guidance weight further increases.

**Dynamic thresholding** : We introduce a new _dynamic thresholding_ method: at each sampling step we set _s_ to a certain percentile absolute pixel value in **x** ˆ _[t]_ 0[,][and][if] _[s][>]_[1][,][then][we][threshold] **[x]**[ˆ] _[t]_ 0 to the range [ _−s, s_ ] and then divide by _s_ . Dynamic thresholding pushes saturated pixels (those near -1 and 1) inwards, thereby actively preventing pixels from saturation at each step. We find that dynamic thresholding results in significantly better photorealism as well as better image-text alignment, especially when using very large guidance weights.

4

## **2.4 Robust cascaded diffusion models**

Imagen utilizes a pipeline of a base 64 _×_ 64 model, and two text-conditional super-resolution diffusion models to upsample a 64 _×_ 64 generated image into a 256 _×_ 256 image, and then to 1024 _×_ 1024 image. Cascaded diffusion models with noise conditioning augmentation [29] have been extremely effective in progressively generating high-fidelity images. Furthermore, making the super-resolution models aware of the amount of noise added, via noise level conditioning, significantly improves the sample quality and helps improving the robustness of the super-resolution models to handle artifacts generated by lower resolution models [29]. Imagen uses noise conditioning augmentation for both the super-resolution models. We find this to be a critical for generating high fidelity images.

Given a conditioning low-resolution image and augmentation level (a.k.a aug_level) (e.g., strength of Gaussian noise or blur), we corrupt the low-resolution image with the augmentation (corresponding to aug_level), and condition the diffusion model on aug_level. During training, aug_level is chosen randomly, while during inference, we sweep over its different values to find the best sample quality. In our case, we use Gaussian noise as a form of augmentation, and apply variance preserving Gaussian noise augmentation resembling the forward process used in diffusion models (Appendix A). The augmentation level is specified using aug_level _∈_ [0 _,_ 1]. See Fig. A.32 for reference pseudocode.

## **2.5 Neural network architecture**

**Base model** : We adapt the U-Net [60] architecture from [42] for our base 64 _×_ 64 text-to-image diffusion model. The network is conditioned on text embeddings via a pooled embedding vector, added to the diffusion timestep embedding similar to the class embedding conditioning method used in [16, 29]. We further condition on the entire sequence of text embeddings by adding cross attention [59] over the text embeddings at multiple resolutions. We study various methods of text conditioning in Appendix D.3.1. Furthermore, we found Layer Normalization [2] for text embeddings in the attention and pooling layers to help considerably improve performance.

**Super-resolution models** : For 64 _×_ 64 _→_ 256 _×_ 256 super-resolution, we use the U-Net model adapted from [42, 61]. We make several modifications to this U-Net model for improving memory efficiency, inference time and convergence speed (our variant is 2-3x faster in steps/second over the U-Net used in [42, 61]). We call this variant _Efficient U-Net_ (See Appendix B.1 for more details and comparisons). Our 256 _×_ 256 _→_ 1024 _×_ 1024 super-resolution model trains on 64 _×_ 64 _→_ 256 _×_ 256 crops of the 1024 _×_ 1024 image. To facilitate this, we remove the self-attention layers, however we keep the text cross-attention layers which we found to be critical. During inference, the model receives the full 256 _×_ 256 low-resolution images as inputs, and returns upsampled 1024 _×_ 1024 images as outputs. Note that we use text cross attention for both our super-resolution models.

## **3 Evaluating Text-to-Image Models**

The COCO [38] validation set is the standard benchmark for evaluating text-to-image models for both the supervised [85, 22] and the zero-shot setting [55, 43]. The key automated performance metrics used are FID [26] to measure image fidelity, and CLIP score [25, 51] to measure image-text alignment. Consistent with previous works, we report zero-shot FID-30K, for which 30K prompts are drawn randomly from the validation set, and the model samples generated on these prompts are compared with reference images from the full validation set. Since guidance weight is an important ingredient to control image quality and text alignment, we report most of our ablation results using trade-off (or _pareto_ ) curves between CLIP and FID scores across a range of guidance weights.

Both FID and CLIP scores have limitations, for example FID is not fully aligned with perceptual quality [44], and CLIP is ineffective at counting [51]. Due to these limitations, we use human evaluation to assess image quality and caption similarity, with ground truth reference caption-image pairs as a baseline. We use two experimental paradigms:

1. To probe image quality, the rater is asked to select between the model generation and reference image using the question: “Which image is more photorealistic (looks more real)?”. We report the percentage of times raters choose model generations over reference images (the _preference rate_ ).

2. To probe alignment, human raters are shown an image and a prompt and asked “Does the caption accurately describe the above image?”. They must respond with “yes”, “somewhat”, or “no”. These responses are scored as 100, 50, and 0, respectively. These ratings are obtained independently for model samples and reference images, and both are reported.

For both cases we use 200 randomly chosen image-caption pairs from the COCO validation set. Subjects were shown batches of 50 images. We also used interleaved “control" trials, and only include rater data from those who correctly answered at least 80% of the control questions. This netted 73 and 51 ratings per image for image quality and image-text alignment evaluations, respectively.

**DrawBench** : While COCO is a valuable benchmark, it is increasingly clear that it has a limited spectrum of prompts that do not readily provide insight into differences between models (e.g., see Sec. 4.2). Recent work by [10] proposed a new evaluation set called PaintSkills to systematically evaluate visual reasoning skills and social biases beyond COCO. With similar motivation, we introduce _DrawBench_ , a comprehensive and challenging set of prompts that support the evaluation and comparison of text-to-image models. DrawBench contains 11 categories of prompts, testing different capabilities of models such as the ability to faithfully render different colors, numbers of objects, spatial relations, text in the scene, and unusual interactions between objects. Categories also include complex prompts, including long, intricate textual descriptions, rare words, and also misspelled prompts. We also include sets of prompts collected from DALL-E [55], Gary Marcus et al. [40] and Reddit. Across these 11 categories, DrawBench comprises 200 prompts in total, striking a good balance between the desire for a large, comprehensive dataset, and small enough that human evaluation remains feasible. (Appendix C provides a more detailed description of DrawBench. Fig. 2 shows example prompts from DrawBench with Imagen samples.)

We use DrawBench to directly compare different models. To this end, human raters are presented with two sets of images, one from Model A and one from Model B, each of which has 8 samples. Human raters are asked to compare Model A and Model B on sample fidelity and image-text alignment. They respond with one of three choices: Prefer Model A; Indifferent; or Prefer Model B.

## **4 Experiments**

Section 4.1 describes training details, Sections 4.2 and 4.3 analyze results on MS-COCO and DrawBench, and Section 4.4 summarizes our ablation studies and key findings. For all experiments below, the images are fair random samples from Imagen with no post-processing or re-ranking.

## **4.1 Training details**

Unless specified, we train a 2B parameter model for the 64 _×_ 64 text-to-image synthesis, and 600M and 400M parameter models for 64 _×_ 64 _→_ 256 _×_ 256 and 256 _×_ 256 _→_ 1024 _×_ 1024 for superresolution respectively. We use a batch size of 2048 and 2.5M training steps for all models. We use 256 TPU-v4 chips for our base 64 _×_ 64 model, and 128 TPU-v4 chips for both super-resolution

We use Adafactor for our base 64 _×_ 64 model, because initial comparisons with Adam suggested similar performance with much smaller memory footprint for Adafactor. For superresolution models, we use Adam as we found Adafactor to hurt model quality in our initial ablations. For classifier-free guidance, we joint-train unconditionally via zeroing out the text embeddings with 10% probability for all three models. We train on a combination of internal datasets, with _≈_ 460M image-text pairs, and the publicly available LAION-400M dataset [64], with _≈_ 400M image-text pairs. There are limitations in our training data, and we refer the reader to Section 6 for details. See Appendix F for more implementation details.

## **4.2 Results on COCO**

We evaluate Imagen on the COCO validation set using FID score, similar to [55, 43]. Table 1 displays the results. Imagen achieves state of the art _zero-shot_ FID on COCO at 7.27, outperforming the concurrent work of DALL-E 2 [56] and even models trained on COCO. Table 2 reports the human evaluation to test image quality and alignment on the COCO validation set. We report results on the original COCO validation set, as well as a filtered version in which all reference data with people have been removed. For photorealism, Imagen achieves 39.2% preference rate indicating high image quality generation. On the set with no people, there is a boost in preference rate of Imagen to 43.6%, indicating Imagen’s limited ability to generate photorealistic people. On caption similarity, Imagen’s score is on-par with the original reference images, suggesting Imagen’s ability to generate images that align well with COCO captions.

## **4.3 Results on DrawBench**

Using DrawBench, we compare Imagen with DALL-E 2 (the public version) [56], GLIDE [43], Latent Diffusion [59], and CLIP-guided VQ-GAN [12]. Fig. 3 shows the human evaluation results for pairwise comparison of Imagen with each of the three models. We report the percentage of time raters prefer Model A, Model B, or are indifferent for both image fidelity and image-text alignment. We aggregate the scores across all the categories and raters. We find the human raters to exceedingly prefer Imagen over all others models in both image-text alignment and image fidelity. We refer the reader to Appendix E for a more detailed category wise comparison and qualitative comparison.

## **4.4 Analysis of Imagen**

For a detailed analysis of Imagen see Appendix D. Key findings are discussed in Fig. 4 and below.

**Scaling text encoder size is extremely effective.** We observe that scaling the size of the text encoder leads to consistent improvement in both image-text alignment and image fidelity. Imagen trained with our largest text encoder, T5-XXL (4.6B parameters), yields the best results (Fig. 4a).

**Scaling text encoder size is more important than U-Net size.** While scaling the size of the diffusion model U-Net improves sample quality, we found scaling the text encoder size to be significantly more impactful than the U-Net size (Fig. 4b).

**Dynamic thresholding is critical.** We show that dynamic thresholding results in samples with significantly better photorealism and alignment with text, over static or no thresholding, especially under the presence of large classifier-free guidance weights (Fig. 4c).

**Human raters prefer T5-XXL over CLIP on DrawBench.** The models trained with T5-XXL and CLIP text encoders perform similarly on the COCO validation set in terms of CLIP and FID scores. However, we find that human raters prefer T5-XXL over CLIP on DrawBench across all 11 categories.

**Noise conditioning augmentation is critical.** We show that training the super-resolution models with noise conditioning augmentation leads to better CLIP and FID scores. We also show that noise conditioning augmentation enables stronger text conditioning for the super-resolution model, resulting in improved CLIP and FID scores at higher guidance weights. Adding noise to the low-res image during inference along with the use of large guidance weights allows the super-resolution models to generate diverse upsampled outputs while removing artifacts from the low-res image.

**Text conditioning method is critical.** We observe that conditioning over the sequence of text embeddings with cross attention significantly outperforms simple mean or attention based pooling in both sample fidelity as well as image-text alignment.
**Efficient U-Net is critical.** Our Efficient U-Net implementation uses less memory, converges faster, and has better sample quality with faster inference.

## **5 Related Work**

Diffusion models have seen wide success in image generation [28, 42, 62, 16, 29, 61], outperforming GANs in fidelity and diversity, without training instability and mode collapse issues [6, 16, 29]. Autoregressive models [39], GANs [79, 84], VQ-VAE Transformer-based methods [55, 22], and diffusion models have seen remarkable progress in text-to-image [59, 43, 59], including the concurrent

8

DALL-E 2 [56], which uses a diffusion prior on CLIP text latents and cascaded diffusion models to generate high resolution 1024 _×_ 1024 images; we believe Imagen is much simpler, as Imagen does not need to learn a latent prior, yet achieves better results in both MS-COCO FID and human evaluation on DrawBench. GLIDE [43] also uses cascaded diffusion models for text-to-image, but we use large pretrained frozen language models, which we found to be instrumental to both image fidelity and image-text alignment. XMC-GAN [84] also uses BERT as a text encoder, but we scale to much larger text encoders and demonstrate the effectiveness thereof. The use of cascaded models is also popular throughout the literature [14, 41] and has been used with success in diffusion models to generate high resolution images [16, 29].

## **6 Conclusions, Limitations and Societal Impact**

Imagen showcases the effectiveness of frozen large pretrained language models as text encoders for the text-to-image generation using diffusion models. Our observation that scaling the size of these language models have significantly more impact than scaling the U-Net size on overall performance encourages future research directions on exploring even bigger language models as text encoders. Furthermore, through Imagen we re-emphasize the importance of classifier-free guidance, and we introduce dynamic thresholding, which allows usage of much higher guidance weights than seen in previous works. With these novel components, Imagen produces 1024 _×_ 1024 samples with unprecedented photorealism and alignment with text.

Our primary aim with Imagen is to advance research on generative methods, using text-to-image synthesis as a test bed. While end-user applications of generative methods remain largely out of scope, we recognize the potential downstream applications of this research are varied and may impact society in complex ways. On the one hand, generative models have a great potential to complement, extend, and augment human creativity [30]. Text-to-image generation models, in particular, have the potential to extend image-editing capabilities and lead to the development of new tools for creative practitioners. On the other hand, generative methods can be leveraged for malicious purposes, including harassment and misinformation spread [20], and raise many concerns regarding social and cultural exclusion and bias [70, 65, 71]. These considerations inform our decision to not to release code or a public demo. In future work we will explore a framework for responsible externalization that balances the value of external auditing with the risks of unrestricted open-access.

Another ethical challenge relates to the large scale data requirements of text-to-image models, which have have led researchers to rely heavily on large, mostly uncurated, web-scraped datasets. While this approach has enabled rapid algorithmic advances in recent years, datasets of this nature have been critiqued and contested along various ethical dimensions. For example, public and academic discourse regarding appropriate use of public data has raised concerns regarding data subject awareness and consent [24, 18, 63, 45]. Dataset audits have revealed these datasets tend to reflect social stereotypes, oppressive viewpoints, and derogatory, or otherwise harmful, associations to marginalized identity groups [46, 4]. Training text-to-image models on this data risks reproducing these associations and causing significant representational harm that would disproportionately impact individuals and communities already experiencing marginalization, discrimination and exclusion within society. As such, there are a multitude of data challenges that must be addressed before text-to-image models like Imagen can be safely integrated into user-facing applications. While we do not directly address these challenges in this work, an awareness of the limitations of our training data guide our decision not to release Imagen for public use. We strongly caution against the use text-to-image generation methods for any user-facing tools without close care and attention to the contents of the training dataset.

Imagen’s training data was drawn from several pre-existing datasets of image and English alt-text pairs. 400 million examples came from FIT400M, a cleaned version of the Alt-Text dataset [33, 31]. This data was filtered to removed noise and undesirable content, such as pornographic imagery and toxic language. However, a recent audit of another one of our data sources, LAION-400M [64], uncovered a wide range of inappropriate content including pornographic imagery, racist slurs, and harmful social stereotypes [4]. This finding informs our assessment that Imagen is not suitable for public use at this time and also demonstrates the value of rigorous dataset audits and comprehensive dataset documentation (e.g. [23, 47]) in informing consequent decisions about the model’s appropriate and safe use. Imagen also relies on text encoders trained on uncurated web-scale data, and thus inherits the social biases and limitations of large language models [5, 3, 52].
While we leave an in-depth empirical analysis of social and cultural biases encoded by Imagen to future work, our small scale internal assessments reveal several limitations that guide our decision

9

not to release Imagen at this time. First, all generative models, including Imagen, Imagen, may run into danger of dropping modes of the data distribution, which may further compound the social consequence of dataset bias. Second, Imagen exhibits serious limitations when generating images depicting people. Our human evaluations found Imagen obtains significantly higher preference rates when evaluated on images that do not portray people, indicating a degradation in image fidelity. Finally, our preliminary assessment also suggests Imagen encodes several social biases and stereotypes, including an overall bias towards generating images of people with lighter skin tones and a tendency for images portraying different professions to align with Western gender stereotypes. Even when we focus generations away from people, our preliminary analysis indicates Imagen encodes a range of social and cultural biases when generating images of activities, events, and objects.

While there has been extensive work auditing image-to-text and image labeling models for forms of social bias (e.g. [8, 9, 71]), there has been comparatively less work on social bias evaluation methods for text-to-image models, with the recent exception of [10]. We believe this is a critical avenue for future research and we intend to explore benchmark evaluations for social and cultural bias in future work—for example, exploring whether it is possible to generalize the normalized pointwise mutual information metric [1] to the measurement of biases in image generation models. There is also a great need to develop a conceptual vocabulary around potential harms of text-to-image models that could guide the development of evaluation metrics and inform responsible model release. We aim to address these challenges in future work.

progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Progressive (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: This paper presents a compelling and technically sophisticated advancement in text-to-image synthesis through the introduction of Imagen, a diffusion-based model that leverages large language models (LLMs) pretrained on text-only data for superior text encoding. The work is notable for its clear articulation of a key insight—namely, that frozen, off-the-shelf LLMs like T5-XXL are remarkably effective for text-to-image generation when paired with diffusion models, outperforming multimodal encoders such as those used in CLIP. This observation is rigorously supported by empirical evidence, including state-of-the-art performance on COCO (FID 7.27) and strong human evaluation results showing parity with real data in image-text alignment. The paper is well-structured and clearly written, with logical flow and sufficient methodological detail to convey the novelty of components such as dynamic thresholding and Efficient U-Net. However, a significant limitation lies in the lack of critical discussion around ethical implications and potential misuse of such high-fidelity generative models, especially given their photorealistic output capabilities. Additionally, while DrawBench is a valuable contribution, the reliance on human raters without detailed annotation protocols or inter-rater reliability metrics raises questions about reproducibility and objectivity in evaluation. The omission of ablation studies quantifying the relative contribution of each proposed component (e.g., dynamic thresholding vs. language model scale) also weakens causal claims about what drives performance gains. Despite these concerns, the paper represents a high-quality, impactful contribution to the field, advancing both the technical frontier and evaluation standards in generative multimodal modeling.

comments.title: Missing specification of percentile in dynamic thresholding
comments.quote: We introduce a new _dynamic thresholding_ method: at each sampling step we set _s_ to a certain percentile absolute pixel value in **x** ˆ _[t]_ 0[,][and][if] _[s][>]_[1][,][then][we][threshold] **[x]**[ˆ] _[t]_ 0 to the range [ _−s, s_ ] and then divide by _s_.
comments.explanation: The authors introduce dynamic thresholding as a key innovation enabling high guidance weights without saturation artifacts. The method is promising and conceptually sound: rescaling based on a percentile of absolute pixel values prevents extreme values while preserving dynamic range. However, the 'certain percentile' is never specified in the main text or even in Appendix B or A.31 (referenced for implementation). This is a critical omission: the choice of percentile (e.g., 99.5 vs. 95) has a large impact on behavior. Without this value, the method is underspecified and not reproducible. While it might appear in later code releases or supplementary material, within the paper context, this parameter is left implicit. This qualifies as a real gap in method description, especially since static thresholding is clearly defined ([−1,1] clipping), but dynamic thresholding lacks the same precision.

comments.title: Ambiguity in noise conditioning during inference
comments.quote: During training, aug_level is chosen randomly, while during inference, we sweep over its different values to find the best sample quality.
comments.explanation: This statement describes a critical design choice in the super-resolution pipeline: noise-level conditioning via aug_level. The method is sound and builds on prior work [29], but the inference procedure is underspecified. 'Sweep over its different values' suggests a grid search over aug_level ∈ [0,1], but what resolution? Do they sample once per value and pick the best? How is 'best' determined — human evaluation, FID, CLIP score? Is this sweep done per image or globally? This affects reproducibility and computational cost. While some might infer that this is a tuning step done offline or validated on a pilot set, the lack of detail undermines methodological clarity. Since noise conditioning directly impacts image quality and robustness, this is more than a minor detail. However, given that the rest of the architecture is well-specified, and this step likely doesn’t affect core claims, it remains a localized gap.

comments.title: Ambiguous reference to 'higher preference rates' without specifying baseline
comments.quote: Our human evaluations found Imagen obtains significantly higher preference rates when evaluated on images that do not portray people, indicating a degradation in image fidelity.
comments.explanation: Initially, I was confused by the claim that 'higher preference rates' indicate a 'degradation in image fidelity'. Normally, higher preference rates suggest better quality, so this seems contradictory. I checked the definition of 'preference rate' in the PAPER SUMMARY: it is defined as the percentage of times human raters choose model-generated images over real COCO images for photorealism. Thus, a higher rate means raters judge the generated images as more photorealistic than real ones. However, the sentence claims this indicates degradation when people are excluded—implying that performance is worse when people *are* present. The intended meaning seems to be: Imagen scores *lower* in photorealism (i.e., lower preference rate) on prompts *with* people, hence performs worse on human depictions. But the phrasing says the opposite: it states Imagen gets 'higher preference rates' on no-people prompts (which is fine), then interprets that as 'indicating a degradation in image fidelity'—but degradation relative to what? If anything, higher preference rates suggest *better* fidelity on those prompts. The issue is that the causal interpretation is flipped: it should say that lower preference rates on prompts with people reveal degradation in fidelity for human depictions. The ambiguity could mislead readers into thinking high preference rates are bad. Context helps somewhat, but the logic flow is reversed and remains problematic without rephrasing.

comments.title: Unclear justification for freezing large language models
comments.quote: For simplicity, we freeze the weights of these text encoders. Freezing has several advantages such as offline computation of embeddings, resulting in negligible computation or memory footprint during training of the textto-image model.
comments.explanation: The authors justify freezing the T5 encoder primarily on computational grounds: enabling offline embedding computation. This is valid and practical. However, given that the central claim is the superiority of large pretrained LMs (like T5-XXL) for image synthesis, one might expect a discussion of *whether* fine-tuning could further improve performance. The statement 'we find that there is a clear conviction that scaling the text encoder size improves...' implies scaling *without* fine-tuning, but the paper does not compare frozen vs. fine-tuned variants beyond noting that 'there is no need to finetune for strong performance'. This is a missed opportunity: the claim would be stronger if they showed that scaling outperforms fine-tuning, or that fine-tuning offers marginal benefit. As written, readers may wonder if freezing limits potential gains. While not a technical error, this is a mild overclaim — the conclusion that frozen models are sufficient rests on ablation within their setup, but the absence of fine-tuning experiments in the regime of large LMs (e.g., PaLM or Flan-T5-XXL) leaves the boundary of this finding somewhat underspecified.

comments.title: Inconsistent phrasing in key discovery about language model scaling
comments.quote: Our key discovery is that generic large language models (e.g. T5), pretrained on text-only corpora, are surprisingly effective at encoding text for image synthesis: increasing the size of the language model in Imagen boosts both sample fidelity and image-text alignment much more than increasing the size of the image diffusion model.
comments.explanation: Initially, this claim appears strong and well-supported by the paper's ablation studies. The authors convincingly show in Section 4.4 and Appendix D.1 that scaling the T5 encoder (e.g., from T5-Base to T5-XXL) yields larger gains than scaling the diffusion model. However, the phrasing 'much more than increasing the size of the image diffusion model' risks overstatement. While the trend is clear, the paper does not quantitatively compare *relative* scaling — for instance, whether a 10x increase in language model parameters has a larger effect than a 10x increase in diffusion model parameters — nor does it rule out interaction effects or optimal regimes where diffusion model scaling might catch up. The claim is broadly valid given the reported results, but it could mislead a reader into thinking diffusion model scaling is negligible, which the data do not fully support. A more precise phrasing would acknowledge this is an empirical finding under their specific scaling directions and budget constraints.

comments.title: Potential overstatement of human evaluation baseline
comments.quote: human raters find Imagen samples to be on par with COCO real images in image-text alignment
comments.explanation: This claim appears multiple times (Abstract, Introduction, Table 2 caption). In Table 2, the 'Alignment' metric gives Imagen 92.1 ± 0.55 vs. 'Original' (COCO) at 92.2 ± 0.54, which supports 'on par'. However, the phrasing 'on par with the COCO data itself' could be misread as saying Imagen matches or exceeds real images in *overall quality*, but the context clarifies it refers specifically to alignment. Still, in isolation, this risks overstating: the metric is rater agreement with the prompt, not a comparison of generated vs. real image quality. Moreover, the high scores suggest ceiling effects—raters may struggle to distinguish minor flaws—so small differences might not reflect true parity. The authors are careful in their experimental design, but the phrasing in abstract/introduction edges toward overclaim if not read in full context.

comments.title: Overstatement of 'natural to explore both families' without ablation
comments.quote: It thus becomes natural to explore both families of text encoders for the text-to-image task. Imagen explores pretrained text encoders: BERT [15], T5 [53] and CLIP [48].
comments.explanation: The passage asserts that it is 'natural' to explore both image-text pretrained (e.g., CLIP) and text-only pretrained (e.g., T5, BERT) encoders, which is reasonable. However, it presents this as a discovery-motivated decision, while the real contribution — as later shown — is the *superiority* of large text-only LMs over multimodal ones. The phrasing makes it seem like both families are equally plausible starting points, but the paper's key insight is that text-only models *unexpectedly* outperform multimodal ones. This understates the novelty. The claim is not technically wrong, but it misrepresents the intellectual trajectory. A more accurate framing would be that conventional wisdom favors image-text encoders, but the authors challenge that by testing large text-only LMs. As it stands, the text makes their hypothesis seem less bold than it actually was. This is a minor issue of framing rather than substance.

comments.title: Inconsistent use of 'Imagen, Imagen' suggests possible duplication error
comments.quote: First, all generative models, including Imagen, Imagen, may run into danger of dropping modes of the data distribution
comments.explanation: At first, I thought this might be a typographical error where 'Imagen' was accidentally duplicated, possibly during editing. Given that the sentence immediately discusses a general property of generative models ('dropping modes'), it would make sense to refer to Imagen only once as an instance of such models. Checking the surrounding context shows that this is in a subsection discussing limitations and societal impact, where precise phrasing matters for tone and clarity. While the meaning is still recoverable—readers will likely interpret this as a repetition error—the redundant 'Imagen, Imagen' could mislead a careful reader into thinking there's a distinction being made (e.g., different versions of Imagen) or that it reflects an internal model naming convention. OCR errors were considered, but the repetition of the same word makes this unlikely to be a symbol misread (e.g., not a variable confusion). The issue remains problematic as an editorial oversight that affects readability and professionalism, though it doesn't alter technical content.

Standard formulation should be: 

**x̂_t_^{(w)} = w ⋅ x̂_t(c) + (1−w) ⋅ x̂_t(**)**

or in terms of epsilon: **ϵ̂_θ(z_t, c) = ϵ̂_θ(z_t) + w ⋅ (ϵ̂_θ(z_t, c) − ϵ̂_θ(z_t))**. 

The text gives **ϵ_θ := (z_t − αt x_θ) / σt**, which is correct, but the actual guidance-adjusted prediction is missing. The 'ˆ' appears misplaced, possibly due to OCR. While readers familiar with the literature (e.g., Ho & Salimans 2021 [27]) would reconstruct the intent, the formal expression is absent, creating a gap for less experienced readers. However, since this is likely a rendering failure and not an author error (the correct formulation is almost certainly intended), and given that [27] is cited, the omission is forgivable. We do not flag it as a real methodological error, but note that the OCR corruption impairs clarity.

comments.title: Unclear justification for freezing large language models
comments.quote: For simplicity, we freeze the weights of these text encoders. Freezing has several advantages such as offline computation of embeddings, resulting in negligible computation or memory footprint during training of the textto-image model.
comments.explanation: The authors justify freezing the T5 encoder primarily on computational grounds: enabling offline embedding computation. This is valid and practical. However, given that the central claim is the superiority of large pretrained LMs (like T5-XXL) for image synthesis, one might expect a discussion of *whether* fine-tuning could further improve performance. The statement 'we find that there is a clear conviction that scaling the text encoder size improves...' implies scaling *without* fine-tuning, but the paper does not compare frozen vs. fine-tuned variants beyond noting that 'there is no need to finetune for strong performance'. This is a missed opportunity: the claim would be stronger if they showed that scaling outperforms fine-tuning, or that fine-tuning offers marginal benefit. As written, readers may wonder if freezing limits potential gains. While not a technical error, this is a mild overclaim — the conclusion that frozen models are sufficient rests on ablation within their setup, but the absence of fine-tuning experiments in the regime of large LMs (e.g., PaLM or Flan-T5-XXL) leaves the boundary of this finding somewhat underspecified.

comments.title: Incomplete and corrupted equation in diffusion model description
comments.quote:
A diffusion model **x** ˆ _θ_ is trained on a denoising objective of the form

> where _t_ that influence sample quality. ( **x** _,_ **c** ) are data-conditioning pairs,Intuitively, _t ∼U_ **x** ˆ _θ_ ([0is trained to denoise _,_ 1]), _**ϵ** ∼N_ ( **0** _,_ **I** ), and **z** _t_ := _αtα, σt_ **x** _t, w_ + _σt_ are functions of _t_ _**ϵ**_ into **x** using a squared error loss, weighted to emphasize certain values of _t_ .
comments.explanation: The passage attempts to introduce the denoising objective for diffusion models, but the key equation is missing or severely corrupted by OCR artifacts. The text begins with 'A diffusion model **x** ˆ _θ_ is trained on a denoising objective of the form' but instead of presenting a mathematical expression, it immediately jumps into a fragmented sentence. The subsequent prose is garbled, with phrases like '_x_ ˆ _θ_ ([0is trained to denoise' and '_αtα, σt_ **x** _t, w_ + _σt_ are functions of _t_' — the variable '_αtα_' appears to be a misread of '_α_t_', and '_w_' is misplaced. There is no coherent expression of the training objective, such as the standard weighted MSE: _E_{t,x,ϵ}[w_t ||ϵ - ϵ_θ(z_t, c, t)||²]_. While the paper references Appendix A for a precise description, this section is intended as a brief introduction, and as such should present a minimally readable version of the core equation. The surrounding definitions (e.g., z_t) are also mangled, making it impossible to reconstruct the intended meaning from this passage alone. This level of corruption goes beyond acceptable OCR noise and renders the explanation technically unusable.

comments.title: Misleading description of classifier-free guidance sampling formula
comments.quote:
Sampling is performed using the adjusted **x** -prediction

Here,ˆ _**ϵ** θ_ ( **z** _t,_ **c** ) and _**ϵ** θ_ ( **z** _t_ ) are conditional and unconditional _**ϵ**_ -predictions, given by _**ϵ** θ_ := ( **z** _t − αt_ **x** _θ_ ) _/σt_ , and _w_ is the _guidance weight_ .
comments.explanation: The text states that sampling uses an 'adjusted **x**-prediction' but then defines **ϵ**_θ in terms of **x**_θ, which suggests a conflation between **x**-prediction and **ϵ**-prediction parameterizations of diffusion models. The expression _**ϵ**_θ := ( **z_t − α_t x_θ ) / σ_t_ is correct for converting an **x**_θ-model (which predicts clean **x**) into **ϵ**_θ (noise estimate). However, the sampling rule for classifier-free guidance is typically written in terms of **ϵ**_θ as: _**ϵ**_cfg = (1 + w) **ϵ**_θ(**z_t, c**) − w **ϵ**_θ(**z_t, ∅_), and then used to update **z_t**. The text omits this central equation entirely. Instead, it refers to an 'adjusted **x**-prediction' without providing it. Given that the base model uses **x**-prediction (as defined here), the corrected **x**_θ would be: _**x**_cfg = **x**_θ(**z_t, c**) + w(**x**_θ(**z_t, c**) − **x**_θ(**z_t, ∅_)). But this is not stated. The omission makes the sampling procedure underspecified and could mislead readers about how guidance is applied. While experts would know the standard form, the paper fails to complete its own explanation despite introducing all the components.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of dynamic thresholding condition and rescaling
comments.quote: at each sampling step we set _s_ to a certain percentile absolute pixel value in **x** ˆ _[t]_ 0[,][and][if] _[s][>]_[1][,][then][we][threshold] **[x]**[ˆ] _[t]_ 0 to the range [ _−s, s_ ] and then divide by _s_ .
comments.explanation: The description of dynamic thresholding is ambiguous in a way that affects reproducibility. The passage says: 'set _s_ to a certain percentile absolute pixel value' — but does not specify which percentile (e.g., 99.5th is common in such methods). This is a critical hyperparameter. Furthermore, the rescaling step 'divide by _s_' is mentioned, but it's unclear whether this is part of the thresholding operation or a separate normalization. If **x̂** is thresholded to [−s,s] and then divided by s, the output range becomes [−1,1] again — but now with expanded dynamic range. However, this introduces a nonlinearity that could distort the denoising process if not carefully inverted. More importantly, the text says 'if s > 1, then we threshold...', which implies no rescaling when s ≤ 1. But if s ≤ 1, thresholding to [−s,s] would shrink the range, and then dividing by s would amplify it — potentially leading to out-of-bound values again. The conditional logic appears flawed or poorly expressed. A correct implementation likely applies rescaling only when clipping is needed, but this must be explicit. The absence of pseudocode in the main text and the OCR corruption in the quote make this underspecified, even if Appendix A.31 clarifies it.

comments.title: Inconsistent T5 citation across sections
comments.quote: Imagen explores pretrained text encoders: BERT [15], T5 [53] and CLIP [48].
comments.explanation: The abstract and Section 2.1 cite T5 as [53] here, but the earlier reference in the Introduction cites T5 as [54]. Given that [54] is the standard Raffel et al. 2020 'Exploring the Limits of Transfer Learning with a Unified Text-to-Text Transformer' paper (the canonical T5 paper), [53] is likely an OCR error. Indeed, in the sentence just before this one, the authors cite 'T5 [54]' when discussing large language models, so the shift to [53] is inconsistent. While this could be a different paper, the context suggests it's a reference to the same T5 model. The summary also confirms T5 [54] as the correct citation. This is therefore likely an OCR misread of '54' as '53' in one instance. Since citations are not mathematical and the correct paper is clearly intended, this does not affect technical understanding, but it is a minor inconsistency that should be corrected.

comments.title: Overstatement of 'natural to explore both families' without ablation
comments.quote: It thus becomes natural to explore both families of text encoders for the text-to-image task. Imagen explores pretrained text encoders: BERT [15], T5 [53] and CLIP [48].
comments.explanation: The passage asserts that it is 'natural' to explore both image-text pretrained (e.g., CLIP) and text-only pretrained (e.g., T5, BERT) encoders, which is reasonable. However, it presents this as a discovery-motivated decision, while the real contribution — as later shown — is the *superiority* of large text-only LMs over multimodal ones. The phrasing makes it seem like both families are equally plausible starting points, but the paper's key insight is that text-only models *unexpectedly* outperform multimodal ones. This understates the novelty. The claim is not technically wrong, but it misrepresents the intellectual trajectory. A more accurate framing would be that conventional wisdom favors image-text encoders, but the authors challenge that by testing large text-only LMs. As it stands, the text makes their hypothesis seem less bold than it actually was. This is a minor issue of framing rather than substance.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Augmentation Level Specification for Super-Resolution Training
comments.quote: The augmentation level is specified using aug_level ∈ [0 , 1].
comments.explanation: At first glance, this appears to be a straightforward definition: aug_level is a scalar in [0,1] parameterizing the strength of Gaussian noise augmentation. Given the context of noise conditioning augmentation [29] and the reference to Appendix A's forward diffusion process, it's plausible that aug_level corresponds to normalized noise schedule time (e.g., t/T), which would align with common practice. However, the passage does not specify *how* aug_level maps to actual noise levels. Is it linear in variance? In standard deviation? Is it the diffusion timestep index normalized to [0,1]? This mapping is critical for reproducibility because different choices (e.g., uniform in t vs. in \beta_t) yield different training dynamics. While the use of variance-preserving augmentation suggests alignment with the diffusion forward process, the lack of explicit functional form (e.g., \sigma(aug\_level) = \sigma_t where t = aug\_level \cdot T) leaves key implementation details underspecified. The reference to Fig. A.32 for pseudocode suggests this may be clarified later, but as written in the main text, the method is underspecified.

comments.title: Inference-Time Sweep Over aug_level Lacks Specification of Selection Criterion
comments.quote: During inference, we sweep over its different values to find the best sample quality.
comments.explanation: The authors state they sweep over aug_level values during inference to optimize sample quality, which is a reasonable strategy given the stochastic nature of diffusion sampling. However, they do not specify how 'best sample quality' is determined. Is it based on human judgment, automated metrics (FID, CLIP), or some internal model score? In high-stakes evaluation (e.g., reporting state-of-the-art FID), unsupervised sample selection can introduce bias if not carefully controlled. While human evaluation is used later, for automated metrics like FID-30K, selecting samples based on a quality proxy—or even multiple sampling retries—can artifactually improve scores. The paper does not clarify whether this sweep is part of a per-prompt selection mechanism or a fixed hyperparameter tuning step done once. If it's done per prompt, it could constitute re-ranking, but the paper explicitly states 'no post-processing or re-ranking' in Section 4. This creates a potential contradiction. The lack of clarity on whether the sweep is for hyperparameter selection (acceptable) or per-sample selection (potentially problematic) makes this underspecified.

comments.title: Missing Detail on Text Conditioning in Super-Resolution Models
comments.quote: Note that we use text cross attention for both our super-resolution models.
comments.explanation: The authors correctly emphasize the importance of text cross-attention in the 256×256 → 1024×1024 model, especially after removing self-attention layers. However, the description lacks detail on *how* text conditioning is applied. Is the same T5-XXL text embedding used as in the base model? Is it pooled or used as a sequence? Is it conditioned only via cross-attention, or also combined with timestep embeddings? The base model is described with both pooled embedding addition and cross-attention, but no such detail is given for the super-resolution models. Given that text understanding is central to Imagen’s claims, and that robust conditioning is critical in cascaded models, this omission makes it difficult to reproduce the exact architecture. While Appendix B.1 is referenced, the main text should minimally specify if the same conditioning mechanism is used as in the base model.

comments.title: Unclear Mapping Between DrawBench Prompt Examples and Categories
comments.quote:
**----- Start of picture text -----**

A brown bird and a blue bear. One cat and two dogs sitting on the grass. A sign that says ’NeurIPS’.
A small blue book sitting on a large red book. A blue coloured pizza. A wine glass on top of a dog.
A pear cut into seven pieces A photo of a confused grizzly bear A small vessel propelled on water
arranged in a ring. in calculus class. by oars, sails, or an engine.
comments.explanation: The passage presents these prompts as examples from DrawBench (via Fig. 2), and earlier text describes 11 prompt categories including cardinality, spatial relations, and unusual interactions. Some prompts clearly fit categories (e.g., 'A pear cut into seven pieces' tests cardinality, 'A sign that says NeurIPS' tests text-in-scene). However, others are ambiguous: 'A photo of a confused grizzly bear in calculus class' could test implausibility or rare concepts, but without knowing which category it belongs to, it's hard to interpret the evaluation design. While not a fatal flaw, better alignment between example prompts and stated categories would improve clarity. Since the figure is described as showing 'different categories', readers might reasonably expect each line to map to a distinct category, but this is not explicitly confirmed.

comments.title: Potential Overstatement of Human Evaluation Baseline
comments.quote: human raters find Imagen samples to be on par with COCO real images in image-text alignment
comments.explanation: This claim appears multiple times (Abstract, Introduction, Table 2 caption). In Table 2, the 'Alignment' metric gives Imagen 92.1 ± 0.55 vs. 'Original' (COCO) at 92.2 ± 0.54, which supports 'on par'. However, the phrasing 'on par with the COCO data itself' could be misread as saying Imagen matches or exceeds real images in *overall quality*, but the context clarifies it refers specifically to alignment. Still, in isolation, this risks overstating: the metric is rater agreement with the prompt, not a comparison of generated vs. real image quality. Moreover, the high scores suggest ceiling effects—raters may struggle to distinguish minor flaws—so small differences might not reflect true parity. The authors are careful in their experimental design, but the phrasing in abstract/introduction edges toward overclaim if not read in full context.

The column's content then alternates between model names and human evaluation results, but the alignment is broken. The fourth column contains both model names and human evaluation labels (like 'Original', 'No people') as well as photorealism percentages and alignment scores, suggesting that it should be two separate columns: one for the condition ('Original' vs 'No people') and one for the results. However, due to formatting corruption, these are merged. The photorealism and alignment data intended for Imagen are all listed on the same line: 'Imagen

This suggests that the table was meant to have two evaluation splits, but the corruption and poor formatting make it impossible to extract the correct data without refering to Section 4.2. comments.title: Ambiguous reference to 'models' after Table 2
comments.quote: models. We do not find over-fitting to be an issue, and we believe further training might improve overall performance.
comments.explanation: The passage ends abruptly with the word 'models.', which is the truncated beginning of the following sentence in the full paper ('We use Adafactor for our base 64×64 model...'). While this is likely due to the extraction cutoff and not an actual paper flaw, the isolated word 'models.' following a garbled table and preceding a new sentence about training could mislead a reader into thinking this refers to entries in Table 2 — particularly the corrupted sequence that lists model names. However, given the context from the full paper, this is just a truncation artifact. The intended content is clear in the full context: this sentence introduces training details for the diffusion *models*. Since this is clearly a result of text extraction and not an author error, it is not a real issue.

comments.title: Mismatch between text and table on Imagen's FID value
comments.explanation: In Section 4.2, the text states: 'Imagen achieves state of the art _zero-shot_ FID on COCO at 7.27', and Table 1 confirms a zero-shot FID of 7.27. However, in Table 2, the same value (7.27) is listed under a column labeled 'FID-30K' and 'Zero-shot FID-30K' (split across two rows), which could confuse a reader about whether this is FID-10K, FID-30K, or inconsistently reported. The PAPER SUMMARY clarifies that FID-30K is used throughout, and Table 1 confirms that FID-30K is the metric reported. The use of 'FID-30K' and 'Zero-shot FID-30K' interchangeably is consistent with the paper's convention (e.g., 'zero-shot' specifies the evaluation regime, while FID-30K is the metric). The value 7.27 matches across text and table, so this is not a numerical inconsistency. However, the column header formatting — split across two rows — is ambiguous and exacerbated by OCR noise. This doesn't invalidate the claim, but it risks misreading. The authors got it right in content; presentation is the issue.

comments.title: Inconsistent presentation of human evaluation scores in Table 2
comments.explanation:
This row is highly ambiguous: it lists two models (DALL-E [55] and LAFITE [85]), two FID values (17.89 and 26.94), then under the next column 'Original', '50.0%', '91.9±0.42', 'Imagen', '39.5±0.75%', '91.4±0.44'. It appears that 'Original' and '50.0%' (photorealism) and '91.9±0.42' (alignment) are meant for DALL-E, but the row then includes 'Imagen' and another set of scores, likely meant for the same model but under a different caption. However, the previous explanation suggests that Imagen's scores should appear under a separate row. The appearance of 'Imagen' in the results column for other models is likely an OCR artifact. Given that the subsequent row lists 'GLIDE [43]' and only one set of scores, and given that the final row correctly lists 'Imagen (Our Work)' with its own scores, it appears that 'Imagen
39.5...' is a fragmented carryover that corrupted this row. The correct data is recoverable from context: DALL-E [55] has 50.0% photorealism and 91.9 alignment, LAFITE has 26.94 FID, but its human scores are likely in the next row, which states 'LAFITE [85]' again and provides 39.5% and 91.4%. This double-mentioning and score misplacement suggests data formatting error. The PAPER SUMMARY indicates that LAFITE was evaluated separately, so two distinct rows are needed. The merged row misattributes Imagen's scores.

comments.title: Inconsistent use of 'Imagen, Imagen' suggests possible duplication error
comments.quote: First, all generative models, including Imagen, Imagen, may run into danger of dropping modes of the data distribution
comments.explanation: At first, I thought this might be a typographical error where 'Imagen' was accidentally duplicated, possibly during editing. Given that the sentence immediately discusses a general property of generative models ('dropping modes'), it would make sense to refer to Imagen only once as an instance of such models. Checking the surrounding context shows that this is in a subsection discussing limitations and societal impact, where precise phrasing matters for tone and clarity. While the meaning is still recoverable—readers will likely interpret this as a repetition error—the redundant 'Imagen, Imagen' could mislead a careful reader into thinking there's a distinction being made (e.g., different versions of Imagen) or that it reflects an internal model naming convention. OCR errors were considered, but the repetition of the same word makes this unlikely to be a symbol misread (e.g., not a variable confusion). The issue remains problematic as an editorial oversight that affects readability and professionalism, though it doesn't alter technical content.

comments.title: Ambiguous reference to 'higher preference rates' without specifying baseline
comments.quote: Our human evaluations found Imagen obtains significantly higher preference rates when evaluated on images that do not portray people, indicating a degradation in image fidelity.
comments.explanation: Initially, I was confused by the claim that 'higher preference rates' indicate a 'degradation in image fidelity'. Normally, higher preference rates suggest better quality, so this seems contradictory. I checked the definition of 'preference rate' in the PAPER SUMMARY: it is defined as the percentage of times human raters choose model-generated images over real COCO images for photorealism. Thus, a higher rate means raters judge the generated images as more photorealistic than real ones. However, the sentence claims this indicates degradation when people are excluded—implying that performance is worse when people *are* present. The intended meaning seems to be: Imagen scores *lower* in photorealism (i.e., lower preference rate) on prompts *with* people, hence performs worse on human depictions. But the phrasing says the opposite: it states Imagen gets 'higher preference rates' on no-people prompts (which is fine), then interprets that as 'indicating a degradation in image fidelity'—but degradation relative to what? If anything, higher preference rates suggest *better* fidelity on those prompts. The issue is that the causal interpretation is flipped: it should say that lower preference rates on prompts with people reveal degradation in fidelity for human depictions. The ambiguity could mislead readers into thinking high preference rates are bad. Context helps somewhat, but the logic flow is reversed and remains problematic without rephrasing.

comments.title: Underspecified dynamic thresholding implementation in prose description
comments.quote: at each sampling step we set s to a certain percentile absolute pixel value in \mathbf{\hat{x}}_0^{(t)},[and][if] [s][>][1],[then][we][threshold] \mathbf{\hat{x}}_0^{(t)} to the range [-s, s] and then divide by s.
comments.explanation: The method of 'dynamic thresholding' is introduced as a key technical contribution. Initially, I looked to see if all necessary parameters were specified. The passage states that s is set to 'a certain percentile' but does not specify which percentile (e.g., 99.5th, as is common in such methods). While the PAPER SUMMARY mentions 'percentile threshold s', it still doesn't name the value used. In practice, this parameter significantly affects results—too low and you clip too aggressively; too high and saturation persists. The reference to Appendix Fig. A.31 is given, and it's possible the value is defined there, but within the main body and summary context provided, this remains underspecified. The lack of specification violates reproducibility standards in the field: a reader cannot implement this method without knowing the percentile. Other works typically report this value (e.g., Glide used 0.995). This is not a mathematical error, but a methodological omission. The effectiveness of dynamic thresholding may hinge on this choice, so keeping it implicit weakens the contribution. OCR artifacts like '[and][if]' suggest possible corruption, but even with cleaned text, the percentile is missing.

comments.title: Claim about Adafactor vs Adam lacks supporting evidence in text
comments.quote: We use Adafactor for our base 64 \times 64 model, because initial comparisons with
comments.explanation: The sentence is cut off, but in context, it begins to justify the choice of Adafactor over Adam for the base model, citing 'initial comparisons'. This aligns with the PAPER SUMMARY claim that 'Adafactor provides similar performance to Adam with smaller memory footprint'. However, no data or reference is provided in the visible text to support this—no ablation study, table, or figure is cited to show comparable performance with reduced memory. The decision to use Adam for super-resolution models is said to be based on ablation studies showing better quality, which is reasonable. But for Adafactor, the claim of 'similar performance' is asserted without evidence in the given context. While this may be detailed in Appendix D.2 or elsewhere, within the provided passage, it appears undersupported. This becomes an issue because optimization choice affects training dynamics and final model quality—readers need to trust that Adafactor was not a compromise in performance. Given that the claim is key to the system design (memory efficiency enabling larger-scale training), the lack of justification is notable. Context suggests it may be resolved later, but as presented, it's an underspecified rationale.

progressive__glm-4.6.label: Progressive (glm-4.6)
progressive__glm-4.6.overall_feedback: This paper presents a remarkably clear and compelling introduction, exhibiting a high standard of academic writing with a strong, well-motivated thesis and a lucid enumeration of its key contributions. The central claim—that large, frozen language models pretrained on text-only data are exceptionally effective encoders for text-to-image synthesis—is both powerful and convincingly supported by the reported state-of-the-art quantitative (FID) and qualitative (human evaluation) results. The introduction of DrawBench as a new, more challenging evaluation benchmark is a significant and valuable contribution to the field. The most significant issue that the remainder of the paper must address is moving beyond the *what* to the *why*; a deeper analysis is needed to explain the mechanism behind this "surprising" effectiveness. Specifically, the paper would be strengthened by a thorough ablation study dissecting whether the performance gains stem primarily from the sheer scale of the language model, its text-only pre-training objective, or other architectural properties, and how it compares more granularly against multi-modal encoders beyond a simple performance metric. Furthermore, given the impressive results, a critical and detailed discussion of the model's limitations, biases, and failure modes will be essential for a complete and responsible scientific contribution.

comments.explanation: The equations and explanations in this section are completely garbled, making it impossible to understand the specific formulation of the diffusion model used in Imagen. While this appears to be an OCR error, even assuming the most plausible intended equations, the presentation is unclear. The standard denoising objective for diffusion models should be something like L(θ) = E_{t, x, ε, c} [w_t ||ε - ε_θ(z_t, c)||^2], where t ~ U[0, 1], (x, c) are data-conditioning pairs, ε ~ N(0, I) is noise, z_t = α_t x + σ_t ε is the noisy data at time t, ε_θ is the noise prediction model, and w_t are weights. The sampling process should start from pure noise z_1 ~ N(0, I) and iteratively generate points z_1, ..., z_T, where x = z_0 is the final image. But the text doesn't clearly present these equations, and the notation is all mixed up. This is a major issue because it affects the understanding of the paper's methodology.

comments.title: Underspecified Dynamic Thresholding Method
comments.quote: We introduce a new dynamic thresholding method: at each sampling step we set s to a certain percentile absolute pixel value in x̂_0[t], and if s > 1, then we threshold x̂_0[t] to the range [-s, s] and then divide by s.
comments.explanation: The authors introduce dynamic thresholding as a key contribution but don't specify which percentile to use for the parameter s. This is a critical parameter that would significantly affect the performance and behavior of the method. Without specifying this value, the method is underspecified and difficult to reproduce. The authors refer to Appendix Fig. A.31 for a reference implementation, but even there, the percentile value is not mentioned in the provided text. Different percentile values (e.g., 90th, 95th, 99th) would lead to different behaviors of the algorithm.

progressive_original__glm-4.6.label: Progressive Original (glm-4.6)
progressive_original__glm-4.6.overall_feedback: This paper presents a remarkably clear and compelling introduction, exhibiting a high standard of academic writing with a strong, well-motivated thesis and a lucid enumeration of its key contributions. The central claim—that large, frozen language models pretrained on text-only data are exceptionally effective encoders for text-to-image synthesis—is both powerful and convincingly supported by the reported state-of-the-art quantitative (FID) and qualitative (human evaluation) results. The introduction of DrawBench as a new, more challenging evaluation benchmark is a significant and valuable contribution to the field. The most significant issue that the remainder of the paper must address is moving beyond the *what* to the *why*; a deeper analysis is needed to explain the mechanism behind this "surprising" effectiveness. Specifically, the paper would be strengthened by a thorough ablation study dissecting whether the performance gains stem primarily from the sheer scale of the language model, its text-only pre-training objective, or other architectural properties, and how it compares more granularly against multi-modal encoders beyond a simple performance metric. Furthermore, given the impressive results, a critical and detailed discussion of the model's limitations, biases, and failure modes will be essential for a complete and responsible scientific contribution.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: This paper serves as a high-impact technical report that effectively bridges the gap between large-scale language modeling (LLM) and generative image synthesis. The authors present a compelling, well-structured argument that shifts the focus from image-based training objectives to the transformative power of frozen, pre-trained text encoders, justifying this shift with strong empirical evidence, including state-of-the-art FID scores and a new, robust evaluation framework (DrawBench). The clarity is exceptional; the hierarchical presentation—from the high-level intuition provided in the introduction to the methodical breakdown of components like dynamic thresholding and the Efficient U-Net—makes the complex pipeline accessible to a broad academic audience. A potential point of critical scrutiny, however, is the reliance on proprietary, massive-scale compute and private datasets; while the methodological contributions are significant, the paper faces the common challenge of reproducibility for researchers without access to Google-scale infrastructure. Overall, the work is highly persuasive, framing the scaling of language models as the primary driver for improved image-text alignment, thus setting a new standard for the field.

progressive_original__gemini-3.1-flash-l.overall_feedback: This paper serves as a high-impact technical report that effectively bridges the gap between large-scale language modeling (LLM) and generative image synthesis. The authors present a compelling, well-structured argument that shifts the focus from image-based training objectives to the transformative power of frozen, pre-trained text encoders, justifying this shift with strong empirical evidence, including state-of-the-art FID scores and a new, robust evaluation framework (DrawBench). The clarity is exceptional; the hierarchical presentation—from the high-level intuition provided in the introduction to the methodical breakdown of components like dynamic thresholding and the Efficient U-Net—makes the complex pipeline accessible to a broad academic audience. A potential point of critical scrutiny, however, is the reliance on proprietary, massive-scale compute and private datasets; while the methodological contributions are significant, the paper faces the common challenge of reproducibility for researchers without access to Google-scale infrastructure. Overall, the work is highly persuasive, framing the scaling of language models as the primary driver for improved image-text alignment, thus setting a new standard for the field.

comments.explanation: The text in Section 2.2 contains significant OCR-related corruption (e.g., 't that influence sample quality', 'z t := αtα, σt x t, w + σt are functions of t'). While I can infer the authors intended to define the denoising objective and the forward process (diffusion) relation $z_t = \alpha_t x + \sigma_t \epsilon$, the notation has bled into the prose in a way that renders the formal definitions unreadable.
